items: Complete a written baseline inventory covering your cat's activity patterns, food relationships, sleep locations, grooming behavior, and social navigation habits right now.
Identify and record specific deviations from your cat's normal behavior patterns since sudden aggression without environmental change signals something internal has shifted.
Track your cat's movement preferences including preferred height, territorial choices, abandonment of favorite perches, and any sudden changes in her chosen spaces over time.
Document how your cat responds to touch including where she solicits contact versus where she rejects it so tolerance changes become measurable data for your vet.
Schedule a veterinary appointment specifically to rule out pain-related causes and prepare a complete medical timeline covering aggression triggers, onset dates, and all behavioral changes.
Request diagnostic tests for pain-related causes of aggression rather than accepting reassurance without examination since cats hide pain exceptionally well.
Push back firmly if your veterinarian dismisses sudden behavioral changes as normal aging because sudden aggression always warrants a full medical workup.
Use a science-based reintroduction protocol that respects your cat's fear rather than forcing proximity, as pushing interaction before safety is restored causes more damage.
Monitor your own hormone changes including pregnancy, menstrual cycle shifts, or birth control changes that can alter your body chemistry and trigger defensive cat behavior.
Maintain household stability during periods of your own hormonal fluctuation to prevent compounding stressors that increase cat aggression risk.
Keep in mind that reintroduction takes time measured in weeks, not days, and that forced interaction before safety is restored will reignite aggression episodes.
Accept that cat fights signal an underlying problem between the animals, not bad character, and never punish cats for aggressive behavior rooted in fear or pain.
